speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: You've got two [? sections. ?] The first one will be five, the second one will be two.

speaker: interviewee
text: So how did I do? How did I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were great. You were fine. How do you think you're doing? You just tell us--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing great.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to connect the one and the other, because we were talking about my brother, and how I met my husband. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. Interview with Helena Snidman. I just want to take you back to when you were in Lviv, and you were living with a  gentile woman. Can you tell me a little bit about what you were doing when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doin' to make a living. I was on-- on a market. I used to buy used clothes, and I used to sell. And live at that gentile lady, which took me in from the market. She was doing business, too. She was selling her own things. And she took me. She asked me who I am and what I am. So I told her that I'm alone, and I'm looking for my brother. And that's why I came to Lviv. And that I was told that he's around here somewhere. To make a living, I used to buy old clothes and sell it on the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I find my brother, as I say. And we were planning-- he wasn't my husband yet, my husband. And we were planning to go further, to Romania, because he was afraid that the Germans will come close, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940, in the spring. And they-- the Germans advance. So he was afraid that we're going to get caught. And gonna got caught. And the Russians started to load wagons of trains of refugee, and take them out to Siberia. And this Polish woman hid me. Her husband was a policeman. And she didn't allow it. They came after me a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lviv. And she-- she hid me, so I I couldn't get caught. Because as the Germans transport the Jews to concentration camp, the Russian want concentration the refugees. Doesn't matter. You could have been Polish, English, if you are refugee, you-- it's not your home, you were on the train, here you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware of what was happening with the concentration--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were aware. Oh, yes, we read. We had radios between friends, which they-- short-- shortwave radios. And they find out from England, from France what's going on. And then we talk between ourselves. And we knew that the Germans will advance. So we want to go forward, not to get caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any other Jews on the streets or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of Jews. A lot, as I say. Some people recognize me, which I never knew them, that I am Towja Fiszer's daughter. And they told me that my parents are in-- they run away from the ghetto, those people. The ghetto was already, in '40, forming-- forming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were telling me that they've seen my parent. They've seen [INAUDIBLE] and the baby. Did everybody try and stick together, and try and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, everybody stick together and news between us. Sure. To know. And a lot of people went through to Chernivtsi, and from there to Romania. That would be a way to go out from the Mossad-- you know, the underground Israel organization, to smuggle up, to-- to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living and making a living in Lviv, and did you have enough money? How was that for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my father gave me things that I could make money. Like I had a few piece of diamonds he gave me. He gave me some dollars. Because he had that. He didn't send me out naked, and-- He gave me enough that I could live on. And then I've seen an opportunity that I could make some money. So I started buying old clothes and sell it by profit.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you bumped into your brother, was he-- did he have enough money, as well, on him? The same thing. [INTERPOSING VOICES] He used to do the business on the market-- buying and selling, and make profit. And from that, he was living. But when I met him, I shared. My father gave me diamonds, so I sold that. When -when you saw your brother, did he tell you if he tried to get the rest of the family to leave Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. You couldn't, because-- because my father had an occasion. He had a big [INAUDIBLE] machine, a truck. And we got set up for him all comfortable that he could go to the Russian zone. And then my mother went and said goodbye. [SOB] Start crying and he left. He went back. He says, you cry, I don't go. But otherwise, he could save. Because whatever my father did, the whole family did. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] I-- I went back one time on a special occasion. I received a letter from my father that the-- the Germans allow to open up the stores. But it has to be the original owner to sign the license certificate. So I came all the way from Lviv to Warsaw with a guy which-- a guide-- a guide, which took me across. And I went, and I signed the license. And my father didn't want me to leave anymore. So he got angry. He didn't say goodbye. He left and I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you went with your brother, and you met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no I met my husband, myself. But my brother was with us already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Russian-- I was working in Lviv in a theater. I was serving a buffet. And my brother came to have dinner. And I said to him, Mosze, don't go outside, because the Russians are grabbing the refugees. And they sending them to Siberia. And he didn't want to listen to me. I went to work. And he went out, and they grabbed him. They send him away. Later, while we were touring Russia, they told us that he heard that I am touring, at that time, my husband. And he wanna to meet me. So he got shot. This I find out from a friend. When he ran away from the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, from the-- he got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me, when you met your husband, how soon after did you get married? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: A year and a half. We got married the 29th of June, 1941 in Lviv. Just no man-- no no wedding. We-- just a man performed at the the the wedding ceremony. And two days after, we left for Romania, for Chernivtsi. And in Chernivtsi, when we arrived was again the-- the meeting place from all refugees was in cafe houses. So all--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: --all the whispers, and all the news, what's goin' on in the world-- one had a radio. One had this. One had that. That we better proceed further, because the Germans already declared war on Russia on the first of July, two days after we arrive. And my husband knew Russian. So he had a very good position in-- in the government. Because it was announced in a paper, who knows Russian to come to this and this place, that they can help us make a living. So-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband went and he got a very good position with his Russian language. And I kept on working in that cafeteria in the theater-- in the Grand Opera in Lviv, in the cafeteria. I wasn't an opera singer. [LAUGHS] Now, you said that this was after Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: After what?

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after you left Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, this is in Romania. Chernivtsi.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Chernivtsi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chernivtsi what belonged at that time to Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russian, the Russian were there. That's why we went there. And after the-- the Germans declared together war on Russia, we proceed further to Samarkand.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Chernivtsi, where did you stay? Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed at the one Dr. [?Kone?]. He came to the cafeteria, and he ask who needs a-- a place to live. So I say, me. And he liked me, so he took me in. And he gave us a room, me and my husband. I even know the-- the, in Romanian, the number-- 11 [NON-ENGLISH]. [SOB] Dr. [?Kone?].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that all the-- the Jewish people would get together at the cafe houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cafe houses and talking, what's goin' on. And the one helped the other. I make money. My husband make money. So the ones that they didn't make it, we fed them. We helped them out. That's the only way you could survive, because some-- some couldn't do the Russian like language like my husband did. And he worked on a very good position, so he make good money. So we helped out the other poor people. In the cafe house, I used to buy them lunches, dinners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish observance, when holidays came around? Did you celebrate? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we didn't even know when. We didn't know when. Until we arrived in Samarkand. And in Samarkand, there are Bukharan Jews. They celebrate like Passover. But we didn't belong to no shul. We used to go out there, because our heart was with it. They were Jewish people. And as a matter of fact, that one of those Bukhara Jewish lady took us in, me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Samarkand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Chernivtsi, in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Romania for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Til-- how long we were in Romania? I don't remember exactly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe six, seven month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Romania, what did you hear about the war outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we heard-- we heard news. As I say, we came together in cafeterias, and we heard the news that the Germans declared war on Russia. There was a radio. And so my husband says, it's no use of staying here. We have to proceed. So we proceed further down. And since we didn't have much clothes to wear, because we sold everything to live on, we want to go to a climate where it's warm. So we went to Samarkand, which is middle Asia. It's a warm climate. And there, my husband worked on different positions. He always maked a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did he do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first work he did, he was working in a mill, where they produce flour. And he was carry on his back those heavy bags-- those carry them to the-- to the mill, where they grind the-- the seeds. And that was very hard work. But my husband was, at that time, a healthy man. So it didn't hurt him. But he wasn't the only one. There were maybe two dozen refugees working in that mill. Because when you work in the mill, you always was sure you were going to get bread. They were given bread and soup for lunch. So my husband shared with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay in Samarkand?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I say, that Bukhara Jewish woman took us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about her and what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well she was a woman without a husband. Their way of living is more or less like Arabic living. They eat the same bread that they roll and put that [INAUDIBLE] up on the oven, like [NON-ENGLISH] pita, they call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: See how long we know in Canada pita? Since the Israelis start coming. Before, it wasn't known. I didn't know about pita. I never bought them. And I-- I never eat them, either, because I can't digest them. It has to be toasted for me. My stomach got all spoiled, you know, from all that food which we ate. So she was a very kind of a woman. She took us in. And we stayed [INAUDIBLE] til we proceed further from his place of work.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us a buggy, what once was for collecting garbage, with two horses with boxes of documents, that when we travel to Russia, we should deliver to a certain destination those boxes, which we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I just want to go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] From the place where he worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Samarkand did people get together at night again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, get together at night. Yeah. And not only did they got together, one helped the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like my husband was working, and he met an old friend at a [NON-ENGLISH], which he carried down in Dubno from the whole-- his his-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what you may call-- the-- the site from where the [? belong, ?] the [? battery ?] belonged, you know? And he met him. And-- and we used to share our food. We didn't eat a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I let him sleep in my place, until he got sick. And I had to take him to the hospital. And I took him to the hospital, he had typhus. And til next day, two days later I come, they say he died. And he was a very rich man. He had a drug store in Dubno. And died like that. And why be in Samarkand, we develop [? bold ?] malaria. That's why I don't hear well from the high fever. This ear got affected.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the hospital-- Did you go to the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I went to the hospital. Sure. My husband, too. He had typhus. I had typhus. And I had malaria. And he had malaria. And before we came to Samarkand, they assigned us to a kibbutz. And my husband was working in a kibbutz. He was cutting hay for the horses to eat. And then I had a little boy, which died in 1942. He lived only a year and two months. Then I had Rena in Samarkand in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were sick, and you and your husband went to the hospital, how was the medical care, the treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very poor. Very poor. If you wasn't a sick person, you didn't come out alive. Was no medicine. As a matter of fact, that my little boy died because they had no vitamin. He took sick at night, at evening, like maybe 5 o'clock out time. And he start snoring. So I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] I say, to [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is Hebrew. [INTERPOSING VOICES] And I went to the hospital, and I say that look in the throat of the little boy. And she says that he has a sore throat. She sent me home, and she says, make it warm. And give him that tonic to drink. And in the morning, he died. And then we moved out from the kibbutz. And my husband got, again, because he was-- it was a shortage of employees with Russian speaking. They were all in the army. So my husband heard through his [? hat ?] man where he worked in Chernivtsi on this position that a hospital needs an administrator, which can speak--

speaker: interviewer
text: He met him on the street by coincidence. He says, [NON-ENGLISH], [SOB] That is in Russian, if he needs work? He says, yes, I could use a job. So he recommended him a hospital. And my husband become administrator from the groceries in that hospital. So we weren't hungry that time, already, because he stole and brought home. This was in Samarkand?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Agalyk. It's a little town from Samarkand, not too far. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't cars that time. It was a buggy and a horse you could rent and go there. But I had no need, because I had there my husband and Rena.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used to bring home some food. He got to know the-- the head doctor. And he liked my husband very much. So he gave him. He says, you have a child, you have a wife at home. So he gave him some food. What do you think the food was? Cream of wheat, 400 gram bread, some ready-made soup from the hospital. They didn't give you meat and steak. Now when was Rena born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rena was born on 22nd of May, 1944 in Samarkand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that? You went to the hospital to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to the hospital. My husband took me. We were walking, and my husband took me. And then it took a little while. Not so fast. But they delivered the baby. I didn't have to pay anything. I had no money to pay. And I had no clothes, either. Like I mentioned, we sold everything. That's why we went to that hot climate, that whatever we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to my husband, how do I'm going to come home? I have-- I haven't got a dress to wear. And the dress, what I wore when I was pregnant, my husband made that from sacks. Sacks-- sack, where you put in the sack potatoes or raisins. You were in it. You raisin.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he make me a pregnant dress from that sack, you know? Like half sacking. So I start crying when he came to visit me. I say, Michael, how do I'm going to go home? I have no dress. So he went on the bazaar, and bought me a used dress, and washed it, and iron it, and brought me. And for the child, he took his long underwear, and he cut it. And I made diapers. From long underwear?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get clothes for your-- for your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a committee from the joint. This is already '44. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was a committee of the joint that when anybody need it, they helped out. They didn't help you the way you should be helped, but they help something. They gave me some clothes for the baby. And it was warm, so you didn't need bundle up, you know. You had a diaper, which I made from, like I say, those long, long underwear, my husband's. And later, they gave me some diapers, and they gave me some clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were traveling to all these places-- Lviv and Bialystock, did you think that you were going to survive? What was going through your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB] I gave up. I didn't-- I didn't want to run. When we were running with my husband, and the bombs was coming, and I could see the German like I see that light. They were throwing bombs when we were traveling through the journey through the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to my husband, you know what, Michael? I have enough. I don't want to go anymore. So he grab me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a bomb explode not too far from us. Everybody got killed. And us both didn't kill. Destiny. To-- like my grandmother said, that one person would be when a new generation comes. That it is written in the Bible, she was reading to me. And I lived with that all my life. And I tell my children that. And I believe in God. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were traveling to all these places, did you still keep your faith in God during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I talked to God everyday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I still believe in God. My children believe that their father died recently, [SOB] that he is with us. I'll take you to my bedroom and I'll show you. My granddaughter wrote me a thank you note for her bat mitzvah. So she write, Bubbe-- I'll bring you, then I'll show you-- Bubbe, she says, Zayde is with us. He looks at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took the card. And she gave me a teddy bear. And I put it at the side where Zayde used to sleep. He was a very good man. Very giving. A good man. And he saved my life, because I gave up. I didn't want to run anymore. I had no use for my parents. I had no use for my sisters. So what's the use to be alone in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB] But he says, oh no. You're not going to give up, not as long as I'm alive. And he say, and as a matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's-- Let's just stop-

speaker: interviewee
text: --he used to tell me very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: --let's just stop for one-- for one second, please.
